speaker: interviewer
text: Take four of the interview with Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. You were telling us about being picked up and taken to a [? samalager ?] and being questioned. Please continue and explain how you came to be released.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, so-called children from mixed marriages and partners, mixed marriages were released. Actually, there were two types of mixed marriages. One was the so-called mixed marriage. It had one partner Jewish, the other partner not Jewish. Those were the regular mixed marriages. And then they had the so-called privileged mixed marriages. They had two wed partners, one not a Jew, one a Jew, but the children were raised as non-Jews. They didn't have to wear the star, neither had the Jewish partner to wear the star. The regular marriages were either without children or children that they raised Jewish. In that case, the children had to wear the star and the Jewish partner had to wear the star. And those children who were raised Jewish were called Geltungsjuden, meaning they were considered Jews. They had to wear the star and actually had to do the forced labor. That is a main difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in which category did you fit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a so-called Geltungsjuden, had to wear the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother also had to wear the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother did not. My father had to, but not my mother. Had I not been raised Jewish, none of us would have to wear the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who made these fine distinctions? Who, did they investigate how you had been reared, whether you had a formal Jewish education? How did they determine who was being raised as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my case, it was known. My father had been active in the Jewish congregation. We went to the synagogue. How could we have said that was not the case. Yes, it was tried. I know for a fact that some people tried to get out of it. In fact, even at some times, women claimed that their husband wasn't actually the father of the child, that the father was some Aryan. That was tried, too. And it was tried too-- a case actually of a school-- school friend-- went to the same class. And we did the forced labor together, and he actually one day disappeared. Went to the organization and talked-- this was a labor organization, where they could have-- Mischlings were allowed. Lo and behold, he didn't wear the star anymore. And actually, in effect, reverted from Geltungsjuden to Mischling.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm quite sure that the mother, who was well to do, bribed someone to do this. This was well known that you could do this. You found the right person who's changing some documents, that was done. In this one case, I know for sure. But why not? Did you know of any incidents where a mixed marriage was dissolved, where the non-Jew was persuaded to divorce the-- the Jew?  Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know in one case, a regular marriage, no children, the husband was somewhat-- I would almost say probably somewhat mentally handicapped. Anyhow, apparently the woman, the wife, wanted to divorce him. And my mother actually went and told her that you can't do this. If you divorce your husband, he will be deported. I don't-- I don't believe it was successful. They actually had the divorce, and he was deported. In another case, the wife waited until the end of the war, and then there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In fact, I'll give you the name of where the-- the wife waited until the war was over. It was our physician, Dr. Frankel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, he said-- when the care packages came, he saw to it that she got some care packages. You saved my life. They lived themselves apart, as he put it. But he still didn't want a divorce. At the moment the war was over, the divorce, yes. Absolutely. If the-- if the non-Jewish partner died or divorced, the moment that happened, deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which was the safest category to be in? The one that afforded a child for instance of a mixed marriage, the status that offered the greatest protection?

speaker: interviewee
text: The so-called Mischling, the person-- the child that was not raised Jewish and didn't have to wear the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were different categories of Mischling as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was second degree, but actually, the-- they-- they didn't deport, or they were actually not involved. They couldn't serve at certain-- I don't think they took into the army, and they couldn't do this or couldn't do that. But there was no deportation. They didn't have to wear the star, and they got regular ration cards and so on. The important point was whether the child of a mixed marriage was raised Jewish or not. That determined it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in your case, what impact did that have on your family? Did your mother have to do forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, yes. I think towards the end of the war, they had to do forced labor too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So had she been an Aryan, not married to a Jew, she probably wouldn't of had to do forced labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, probably not. At that time, granted, they took all-- the work force was low, and they took women too. But to the best of my knowledge, my mother had to do the forced labor because she was married to a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe she worked at a-- uniform, making uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, did he have to do forced labor as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He actually first, he worked in a factory, and-- and later on he had to clean up after air raids. And I had to do the same. Actually, after we went to the Grosse Hamburger Strasse, Mr. Bruner asked me what are you doing right now. I told him I work at the social service agency. He goes, no, no. That-- starting tomorrow, you report to the labor exchange and you work elsewhere here. Do something really-- some work that has some value for the war. And so I did and I was-- but then they send me to a factory that made the best [? I can. ?] Of course, you never really knew what you were doing. But I believe we did certain materials for balloons, for meteorologic balloons. I know because there was one section where they did meteorology and balloons. And I'm quite sure what we did, there. What we did many of, again and again. But of course, I kept my eyes and ears open and I'm quite sure for those weather balloons what they were, that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if there was any sabotage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my knowledge. At that time, look, you had to-- sabotage probably was possible in assembly. If you had to turn out a little piece of metal, yeah long, you couldn't sabotage very much. Either you did it or you didn't do it. And the moment you didn't do it, then immediately it was obvious. There was hardly any-- we have to sabotage. Yes, that [INAUDIBLE] worked as slowly as we could. But then, on the other hand, the people were quite decent. And had we not reached our norm, they would have been in trouble, and you know what would happen. No, we did what we were supposed to do and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now these were still privately held [INTERPOSING VOICES] enterprises?

speaker: interviewee
text: Privately held enterprises that did war work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everyone in your work brigade was also a Jewish slave laborer?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Jewish. Women and I believe I and another-- actually what happened, my classmate from high school was there too. For some reason, by pure coincidence-- in fact, he was already there. And I came afterwards. Good luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you-- were you fed during the workday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we brought our own stuff, what we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you paid at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were paid. We got paid. Of course, what happened in my father's case, all the money wasn't given to us, but had to be put in a special bank account. And my father was only allowed to take out so and so much. Only for a medical and dental bills was a special distribution, you could get more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this if you had a fund, and you could draw from it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, and if for some reason we didn't-- why did that happen in my case, I believe, at one time one of my father's relatives left my mother some money. It was actually a pitiful sum, but it went over a certain amount. And immediately that happened, the-- the bank account couldn't be accessed freely anymore. But it didn't actually-- I know I got a kind of pay slip, and I knew that the money was put in the bank directly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Jews still allowed to own property at this point? Real estate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I doubt it. [INAUDIBLE] In our case, my father didn't have any. And actually, we didn't. Probably what happened, we had a friend too, but of course, the property was owned now by the non-Jewish partner. But honestly, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have more ability to move around? Was-- was she able to leave the small neighborhood? Did she have to observe curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, much more. Because what happened, that once we worked-- shortly after I left the factory, [? if we can ?] go back to it, there was another regulation. You had to have special passes to use public transportation. We had to have it. My mother, of course, didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was she still continuing to see her-- her family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Even I came along. Even after the star, I [? didn't have ?] the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any regulation about mingling, Jews and Gentiles fraternizing or socializing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably not officially, but I was really careful. What did you do if your neighbor reported you to the local political boss there. You realize in Germany each precinct has a political-- what's called a political boss. A kind of-- they called it [? chrisligter ?], that took care of the political aspect of snooping around. This you had to have some courage to do that, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother able to get assistance or support from her family members to help you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, the moment my father lost his job, my aunt, my mother's sister, took care of me. Absolutely. From socks to hat, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Elfride. I called her Aunt [? Fridel. ?] It was-- actually, we met at night and she gave me food packages. And I remember we met one night, it was pretty dark, side street, I didn't have a star. Lo and behold, we walked together. Who shows up around the corner, the big Nazi of the neighborhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my aunt rose to the occasion. Ah, good evening, Frau what have you, I forgot the name. How are you? Oh, yeah, he's just helping me carrying my-- I remember distinctly. So she gave some sort of justification for your being there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what am I going to do. Packages, I am helping. What could you say? She was absolutely-- regularly we received food packages. And even my mother met with her. But that time, for some reason, I had to do it. I don't know what happened. But anyhow, yes they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your Aunt Elfride's last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dressler. D-R-E-S-S-L-E-R. Her husband was director of the equivalent of General Electric.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a fairly highly placed individual.

speaker: interviewee
text: He actually was a party member, but still as you see, he did everything to aid and abet his wife until he couldn't. But he was fairly high up and had some connections where he could get some extra food and help. Furthermore, they had two country houses, probably the equivalent of [INAUDIBLE]. One in east Berlin, west Berlin. Some of our valuables we stored there. And strangely enough, the east one with the Russians survived. And the other one, well, the Russians came there too, but we found out later on, the Russians didn't take anything. The people who were the landlords of this, they just took it, they claimed that the Russians had done it. But, be that as it may.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did they jeopardize their own safety by assisting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, certainly, yes. If they hadn't been fast on their feet, the [INAUDIBLE] what's it doing here? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your Aunt Elfride's husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Ernst Dressler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ernst?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ernst, yeah, Ernst. And actually, in the beginning he took me out Sundays, in the mornings. He had a car, even before Hitler. And early. And we went out to the suburbs, swimming. And he did an awful lot for me. Not everyone that was a party member was an outright Nazi. Yes, sometimes he and my father argued about the Jews, but he still was very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were laboring under the double burden of being a Jew, or an equivalent of a Jew in Berlin. And what was life in Berlin like during war time for the Gentile population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what usually happened, comes at night, you looked to the sky. Well, it's going be cloudy, we're going to have a quiet night. If there's stars, we're going to have an air raid. And that got worse and worse. In the beginning, only the British during the night. Then the Americans during the day. It was primarily for hours you sat down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have blackout conditions at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. And of course what happened, in apartment houses they kept the air raid shelters separate. The Jews could not sit with the other ones, you see, it was quite--. And one time, in our first apartment, we had to go out on the street to go to the next house, because there was no Jewish-- we were the only Jews in the building. And the next house had several. They had set aside the shelter for the Jews. [INAUDIBLE] Jews either, but they kept us separate.

words.text: ones, you see, it was quite--.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there you're sitting and waiting. What would you do? One time, you'd say are they going to get hit and then again, you felt well, tonight we are really going to get it. Once we were sitting and school was still on, it was after 2 o'clock, the school was late one hour and-- If you got released from the air raid shelter before 2:00?

speaker: interviewee
text: If the all clear came after 2 o'clock, we went to school one and a half hour later. If it came before, we had to show up at the regular time. Now, can you imagine us young people, oh, if it has-- it's quiet now, now we need two more minutes. If the all clear doesn't come now. Sometimes happened, sometimes not.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess school boys are school boys, no matter what.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I wasn't any different. Hooray, we made it [INAUDIBLE]. And of course, when that happened, they had to make up the time. Normally, a class was 45 minutes, or 50 minutes, I don't quite recall. After the all clear came after 2 o'clock, then all class meetings were 30 minutes, you see. And of course, then sometimes you didn't have to account for your homework. For some reason we took advantage of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time frame are we talking about exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was approximately '42. I think '43. '43-- at that time, I worked at that factory. I remember it was '43, I'm quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you aware of what the course of the war, what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had a pretty good idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about the progress of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could read the papers, headlines, radio. We listened to the radio, and suddenly, tatatarata, fanfares, drums, a new report from the front. We did this, we did that. We just occupied this, and so on. But you could tell, at the beginning there were one probably every couple of hours. Later on, it was a couple of days, and then none. And obviously, you could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a way of reading between the lines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes. You could see, read lines. Don't forget, we had the map, and you could tell. And people came back to-- from the front and they told that things were not going as well anymore. And let's face it, [INAUDIBLE], they had memorial services, closed down on movie theaters afterwards. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did rations for Gentiles get progressively tighter as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, reduced proportionally. Absolutely. I remember distinctly the first-- it was '39, the Christmas '39-- oh, boy, all the French cognac and what have you-- or was it '40 I don't-- one of the earliest one there. But of course, that stopped, quite a bit, I assure you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was a-- how did one cope with the stresses of war and knowing that you were in a tenuous position as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, we told the latest jokes, jokes which were circulated not by the Jews, but by the Germans. Actually they had quite a bit of a sense of humor. Rations-- well, when will the war be over? Oh, quite easy. The war will be over when the British eat rats, and we in Germany eat rats [GERMAN]. See, stuff like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I must tell you-- particularly in Berlin, Berlin had very good, very sense of humor. Italy, when they capitulated, then the definition, what is one Italian? A tenor. Two Italians? A gang of thieves. Three Italians? A military retreat. See, stuff like this. We even made fun of the [? hospital ?] song. [INAUDIBLE]. Now, could this be dangerous? Could there be repercussions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course, it's dangerous. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to be careful who you told jokes to or who might have overheard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. You were very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the Jews have their own sub brand of jokes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my knowledge. I just remember those. No, actually probably had-- I don't recall. Those just came to mind, those jokes we picked up. But otherwise, were at the factory, we didn't-- we were so busy, we didn't talk to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were services in Germany? Did the-- did the trolleys run on time? Was mail delivered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was absolutely amazing. They could be bombed out, elevated subway, street cars. We had them within hours, it was running again. It was amazing how they could do it. Well, there was German efficiency. They were very efficient. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made repairs to electrical lines, if that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Actually, the-- unless we got direct hits to the factory, not too much time was left therefore-- I remember the air raid came during the day, we knew that the-- the elevated had been hit. By the time 5, 6 o'clock, we went down, it was already fixed. Yes, perhaps only on one line, and we had to wait a little bit, and it was-- service was irregular. But they had service.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still permitted to ride on-- on transportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but a special pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: A special pass that permitted a Jew to ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they made out to specific individuals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, to the Jew or the Jew of so and so. And they got permission to use whatever it was, subway. In my case, all public transportation conveniences.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was specifically so that you could go to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. That was-- there was a law, no Jew were permitted to use public transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to stand in the rear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankly, when there was seats, we sat down. Rather not, of course, did-- if it was full. And of course, if there was a big air raid and you had to go to work, and people tried to get on the train, the train coming already filled, I have to get on, I have to go to work. You'd rather not try to get in there with your star. Later, I was very lucky with the star. Whenever you took off the star and they caught you, you were in bad trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go out without your star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I did it. What happened once, I was stopped twice. Once by a plainclothes man. He just wondered what is a young fellow doing out of uniform. OK, and I showed him my papers, which exclude me from the army. He let me go. And a second time, I was stopped I think it was a Gestapo. He showed me his badge, Gestapo. He saw my-- he knew darned well that I didn't wear the star. He looked at me-- go. He could have arrested me. Actually, one of my former classmates got arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would have been the consequences?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would have put me in the camp. I hoped to get me out. Oh, who knows what. Deportation-- you don't know. You'd rather not find out. He got out. They got him-- actually the-- the boss for the working crew actually went-- [INAUDIBLE] that he was absolutely needed. They couldn't do the demolition without him. He was an expert in what have you. He got out. He was very lucky, because that particular boss who did it was not very nice to my father. But since he did that, they overlooked it and nothing happened. I wonder what he would have done otherwise. But OK, it happened, twice. I was exceedingly fortunate whatever happened. For instance, when I left the factory one day-- at that time, there were no more lists. They just grabbed people when they probably needed to. I don't know how many Jews. They came around. The SS came around in trucks, collected everyone at the various factories and transported us to another transition camps. Crew, Crew was a nightclub. These huge halls. And they took out the chairs, tables, and used it as collection. And there, they brought everyone in, regardless who it was. And then, they more or less weeded people out. Let's see who we have got here. Lo and behold, they got-- it was a friend, Gerhardt, we worked together in the factory. And they realized we were both Geltungsjuden. And the guy was quite nice. And said OK, good luck. I don't want to see you anymore. Bye-bye, and let us go. Of course, we knew exactly what would happen if we would have stayed there. And there again, bureaucracy. Bureaucracy dictated that children of some people, mixed marriage, either adults or their non-Jewish partner, or children from a mixed marriage, were not to be deported at the time. Here I am. And very often in other countries, or away from Berlin, probably that wouldn't have happened. It was much too cumbersome. Take him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were pretty faithful to the order and to adhering to regulation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, I wouldn't be here. You must say German bureaucracy helped to save my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to pause now to change the tape.
